perturbation.original_text: 10. 1 SAP AFRICA warrants that the SOFTWARE will conform to the functional specifications contained in the DOCUMENTATION on the date of installation of the SOFTWARE and will perform, when in use without material alteration on the DESIGNATED UNIT in accordance with the functional specifications set forth in the DOCUMENTATION.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. 1 SAP AFRICA warrants that the SOFTWARE will generally conform to specifications.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the specific guarantee of conforming to the functional specifications in the DOCUMENTATION and weakens the guarantee to 'generally conform'. This is a significant reduction in the warranty provided, introducing legal risk.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 56 of the Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 56 of the Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008 implies a warranty of quality for goods supplied to consumers in South Africa. By significantly weakening the warranty related to the software's conformance to documentation, this clause contradicts the implied warranty of quality mandated by South African law.
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a specific warranty that the software will conform to the functional specifications in the DOCUMENTATION. The changed text weakens this to a general conformance. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the implied warranty of quality under South African law. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this as a 'significant reduction in the warranty provided, introducing legal risk.' Because the specific guarantee is omitted, and this omission weakens a legally mandated consumer protection, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text:
23 INSURANCE
23. 1 Without limiting SAP AFRICA'S liabilities or responsibilities in terms of the AGREEMENT, SAP AFRICA will provide and maintain insurance to cover its liability and responsibilities in terms of this AGREEMENT.
23. 2 Notwithstanding anything elsewhere contained in the AGREEMENT, SAP AFRICA will provide at least:
23. 3 Insurance in terms of the Compensation for Injuries and Diseases Act, No. 130 of 1993, as amended. SAP AFRICA will upon request from TELKOM submit proof to the satisfaction of TELKOM that it is insured under the Compensation for
- 44 - TELKOM CONTRACT NO. 076C/01 Injuries and Diseases Act by providing TELKOM with adequate proof stating that it has paid all assessments due;
23. 4 Legal liability in respect of claims for death and/or injury to persons or loss of/or damage to third party property;
23. 5 Motor Vehicle Liability Insurance in respect of all motor vehicles brought onto the premises of TELKOM.
perturbation.changed_text:
23 INSURANCE
23. 1 Without limiting SAP AFRICA'S liabilities or responsibilities in terms of the AGREEMENT, SAP AFRICA will consider providing insurance to cover its liability and responsibilities in terms of this AGREEMENT.
23. 2 SAP AFRICA may provide some form of insurance.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the wording from a commitment to provide specific insurance coverages ('will provide at least') to a discretionary statement ('will consider providing insurance' and 'may provide some form'), SAP Africa is no longer bound to provide the legally required insurance coverage. It introduces potential non-compliance with South African labor laws, specifically the Compensation for Injuries and Diseases Act, which mandates certain employer insurance responsibilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Compensation for Occupational Injuries and Diseases Act 130 of 1993 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 80 of the Compensation for Occupational Injuries and Diseases Act 130 of 1993
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 80 of the Compensation for Occupational Injuries and Diseases Act 130 of 1993 requires employers in South Africa to maintain insurance coverage for employee injuries and diseases sustained at work. By changing the language to suggest insurance may be optional, the agreement contradicts the mandatory insurance requirement under this Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Home | SAICA G20 - From Classrooms to Global Impact Find out how the G20 is inspiring us to make a difference. Read more Get the edge today! Register to receive info on dates and courses Book Now Ready to take charge? Let's make it happen. Get Plugged In Now! Read more SAICA Biz Impact podcast Find out more ASA: AccountancySA From army boots to suits - Rekgotsofetse Ledwaba CA(SA) Read more Benefits of joining SAICA Part of Chartered Accountants Worldwide’s global network of 1,8 million CAs and students 60715 Active members and associates around the world 3 Complementary accounting designations 15 Recognition agreements with peer accounting institutes 300+ Industry events hosted each year About SAICA The South African Institute of Chartered Accountants (SAICA), South Africa’s pre-eminent accountancy body, is widely recognised as one of the world’s leading accounting institutes. The institute provides a wide range of support services to more than 50 000 members and associates who are chartered accountants (CAs(SA)), as well as associate general accountants (AGAs(SA)) and accounting technicians (ATs(SA)). Who we are Membership Become a member Do you want to be a business leader, a key financial decision maker or even an entrepreneur? Learn how SAICA can get you there. Why become a member? Our members and associates Making a difference in South Africa's economy and society. Meet our members Member value proposition Making a difference in the economy and society “I am part of a prestigious and valued profession that enables me to participate and make a difference in South Africa and globally, while positively impacting the economy and society.” Member value proposition How can we help you? Support Access the SAICA Member Portal to log all your queries and let us know if you require support Learning SAICA provides learning and development opportunities to ensure our members remain relevant and CPD compliant Networking Through SAICA’s various forums, committees and events, members have an opportunity to network with likeminded individuals across the world Resources Members are kept up to date on matters relating to the profession as well as technical developments via SAICA’s various communication channels Latest from SAICA View more 4 Jun 2025 Press release SAICA confirms organisational review: strategic realignment for greater impact The South African Institute of Chartered Accountants (SAICA) revised its strategy in 2024 and confirms that it has undertaken a review of its Target Operating Model (TOM) to ensure that the organisation is structured to adapt to the rapidly evolving business landscape and deliver on its five-year strategy. The TOM aims to evolve SAICA into a deeply Insight-Driven Organisation (IDO) that accelerates member value, enhances organisational capabilities, remains economically and socially relevant, streamlines some processes and embraces sustainability to operate as a winning organisation. Read more 4 Apr 2025 Press release Improved pass rate and key achievements mark the success of SAICA’s January 2025 IAC Johannesburg, Friday 04 April 2024 – With the release of the results for the January 2025 Initial Assessment of Competence (IAC), formerly known as the Initial Test of Competence (ITC), the South African Institute of Chartered Accountants (SAICA) has announced a 70% overall pass rate. This marks an improvement from the previous year’s overall pass rate of 67%, reflecting the effectiveness of SAICA’s initiatives aimed at supporting candidates. Read more 15 Mar 2025 Press release Treasury takes aim at foreign pensions Section 10(1)(gC) of the Income Tax Act currently exempts, among other things, foreign sourced lump sums, pensions and annuities that are received by or accrue to a resident for past employment outside South Africa, except amounts from South African retirement funds or resident long-term insurers. This exemption was introduced into the Act when South Africa moved to a worldwide basis of taxati
perturbation.justification: The original text commits SAP Africa to providing and maintaining insurance, including specific types like Compensation for Injuries and Diseases Act insurance. The changed text replaces this commitment with discretionary language ('will consider providing insurance' and 'may provide some form'). This directly contradicts the mandate that SAP Africa 'will provide' specific insurance coverages. The Perturbation Explanation highlights potential non-compliance with the Compensation for Injuries and Diseases Act, which mandates certain employer insurance responsibilities. The LLM-generated Law Explanation supports this by stating Section 80 of the Act requires employers to maintain insurance coverage. Even though the snippets are not directly helpful, the legal and perturbation explanations, coupled with the change in wording from 'will provide' to 'may provide', clearly shows a contradiction of the legal obligation to provide insurance.

perturbation.original_text:
15. 1 Any notices to be given to the parties in terms of this AGREEMENT shall be in writing and delivered by hand during ordinary business hours or posted by prepaid registered post or transmitted by fax during normal business hours to the addresses mentioned hereunder, which respective addresses the parties choose as their domicilia citandi et executandi for the delivery or service of all notices, communications or legal processes arising out of this AGREEMENT:
SAP AFRICA : SAP Business Park 1 Woodmead Drive Woodmead, Sandton Attention Managing Director
- 37 - TELKOM CONTRACT NO. 076C/01 Fax: (011)235 6002
TELKOM The Managing Executive Procurement Services P 0 Box 447 Pretoria, 0001 19 th Floor, Telkom Towers North 152 Proes Street, Pretoria Fax: (012)311 6210/Tel: (012)311 6047 or such other address in the Republic of South Africa as either party may choose by written notice to the other from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text:
15. 1 Any notices to be given to the parties in terms of this AGREEMENT may be given in any manner the sending party deems appropriate. The addresses for notice are as follows:
SAP AFRICA : SAP Business Park 1 Woodmead Drive Woodmead, Sandton Attention Managing Director
- 37 - TELKOM CONTRACT NO. 076C/01 Fax: (011)235 6002
TELKOM The Managing Executive Procurement Services P 0 Box 447 Pretoria, 0001 19 th Floor, Telkom Towers North 152 Proes Street, Pretoria Fax: (012)311 6210/Tel: (012)311 6047 or such other address in the Republic of South Africa as either party may choose, without notice to the other from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the specific methods of delivering notices (hand, registered post, fax during business hours) with a vague 'any manner the sending party deems appropriate' and removes the requirement of notification for changes of address. The deletion of specific, reliable methods of notice and the removal of the requirement to notify the other party of a change of address for legal notices introduces significant legal risk, particularly around proper notification, which is often a prerequisite for enforcing contractual rights.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 6 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 6 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008 stipulates requirements for delivering documents and notices to companies, implying reasonable and reliable methods. While the Act doesn't explicitly mandate registered post, the 'any manner' clause, combined with removing the obligation to notify address changes, creates ambiguity and potential for unreliable notification, thereby increasing the risk of non-compliance with the Act's intent and potentially prejudicing a party's legal rights. This also contradicts common law principles around adequate notification in contractual matters.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies exact methods for delivering notices (hand, registered post, fax), which provides a clear and reliable means of communication. The changed text replaces these with 'any manner the sending party deems appropriate,' creating ambiguity and increasing the risk of non-delivery or delayed delivery of important notices. Furthermore, the original contract requires parties to notify each other of address changes. The changed text removes this obligation, increasing the risk that a party may not receive notices sent to a previous address. Because no law snippets were provided, the contradiction assessment is based on the explanation that this change contradicts common law principles around adequate notification in contractual matters and increases the risk of non-compliance. The original text minimizes this risk by requiring specific delivery methods and notification of address changes, while the changed text maximizes it.
